title: Dyeing with pomegranate skins
subtitle: Using dried Punica granatum peel to dye alum-mordanted wool a soft yellow, with an iron after-bath for a deeper olive
excerpt: Dried pomegranate peel is one of the tannin-richest plant dye materials available. On alum-mordanted wool it gives a soft warm yellow; an iron after-bath shifts the result to a muted olive-green. The peel's natural tannin content strengthens the mordant bond rather than replacing it. This tutorial covers the full method from pre-soaking the peel through to wastewater disposal for the iron after-bath.
sourceNotes:
Hummel, J. J. The Dyeing of Textile Fabrics. London: Cassell, 1885. Public domain. Internet Archive. Tannin mordant chemistry, interaction between plant tannin content and alum mordant.

Pellew, Charles Livingston. Dyes and Dyeing. New York, 1913. Public domain. Internet Archive. Mordant-by-fibre colour tables; pomegranate rind dyeing notes for wool.

glossaryTerms.definition: A class of water-soluble polyphenolic compounds present in bark, fruit skins, galls, and leaves. In natural dyeing, tannins improve the bond between fibre and mordant. High-tannin plant materials such as pomegranate rind, oak gall, and sumac can serve as a pre-mordant for cellulose fibres or supplement an alum mordant on wool.

A metallic salt or mineral compound that bonds to both the fibre and the dye molecule, creating a stable bridge between them. Without a mordant, most plant dyes wash out quickly. The choice of mordant affects the final colour: alum gives clear yellows and warm tones; iron shifts colours toward green, grey, or black.

A second dye-bath or modifier bath applied after the main dye-bath is complete. A dissolved iron or copper mineral salt is added to a separate water bath and the dyed fibre is moved into it briefly. The metal ion reacts with the dye molecule already bonded to the fibre, shifting the colour.

Abbreviated as WOF. The dry weight of the fibre being dyed, used as a baseline for calculating how much dye material and mordant to use. A recipe stating 30% WOF alum means 30 g of alum for every 100 g of dry fibre.

A dye extract made by simmering plant material in water, straining out the solids, and using the remaining liquid as the dye bath. Decoctions are used for tough plant materials such as bark chips, dried peel, and roots, where the pigment requires heat to release fully.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Dye-pots are dedicated and never return to food use. Wear gloves, an apron, and closed-toe shoes. Work outdoors or with extraction running.

content.text: Dried pomegranate peel is a high-
wool, the peel gives a soft warm yellow at 50%

(WOF). The colour is not gold, not cream: a yellow with a faint green note, warm and slightly muted. The peel's tannin content works alongside the alum rather than replacing it, strengthening the dye bond and improving light fastness compared with low-tannin yellow sources such as onion skin.

shifts the yellow to a muted olive-green, deepening the colour considerably. The iron reacts with both the tannin and the dye compound, pulling the result well away from yellow. This tutorial covers both the plain alum result and the iron after-bath variation. Because the after-bath uses iron sulphate, the wastewater section addresses foul-drain disposal for the iron bath.

items.name: Wool yarn or fleece, dry weight
Stainless steel dye-pot, dedicated to dyeing
Fine-mesh sieve or muslin for straining
Mordant storage jar for alum solution

content.text: Weigh 50 g of dried pomegranate peel and place in a kilner jar or small bowl. Cover with cold water and leave to soak for 4 to 8 hours, or overnight. The soaking water will turn a tawny orange-brown as the tannins and dye compounds begin dissolving. Transfer the peel and all the soaking liquid to the dye-pot when ready. Pre-soaking reduces the simmering time needed to extract the full colour.

Submerge the wool in cool water and leave for at least 30 minutes before the mordant bath. Pre-wetting ensures the fibre absorbs the mordant solution evenly. Dry fibres take colour unevenly, with patches where the wool first contacted the liquid taking more than the rest. Squeeze gently to remove excess water; do not wring.

Dissolve 25 g of alum potash in 200 ml of hot water in the mordant jar, then stir into a pot of warm water large enough for the wool to move freely without compressing. Lower the pre-wetted wool into the mordant bath. Bring to 80°C over 30 minutes and hold at 80°C for 45 minutes, turning the wool every 10 minutes. Remove the wool and set aside without rinsing. The unmordanted mordant bath can be cooled and stored in the mordant jar for one further use.

Transfer the soaked peel and its liquid to the dye-pot. Top up with enough water to allow the wool to move freely. Bring to 80°C and hold for 45 minutes, pressing the peel occasionally to encourage pigment release. This is the
step. Strain through a fine-mesh sieve or muslin, pressing the peel firmly to extract as much liquid as possible. Discard the spent peel and return the orange-brown liquid to the pot.

Lower the mordanted wool into the strained dye bath. Bring to 80°C over 20 minutes and hold at 80°C for 60 minutes. Turn the wool every 15 minutes to encourage even uptake. Lift a small section after 40 minutes to check the colour: on alum-mordanted wool the result at this point will already show a clear yellow. The colour deepens slightly over the final 20 minutes. Remove the wool once the colour is where you want it.

Lift the wool from the dye bath. Cool slightly before rinsing in water at a similar temperature to avoid felting. Rinse until the water runs clear. Squeeze gently without wringing. Dry in shade away from direct sun. If a plain alum-only result is wanted, the wool is finished here.

To shift the soft yellow to a muted olive-green, prepare a separate after-bath: dissolve 2 g of iron sulphate in 200 ml of hot water, then stir into a pot of warm water at roughly 60°C. Lower the dyed, rinsed wool into the iron bath and hold for 10 to 15 minutes at 60°C, turning once or twice. The colour shift from yellow toward olive happens quickly. Lift and check: the result is a dusky olive-green with a grey cast, quite different from the plain alum yellow. Remove the wool as soon as the colour is where you want it, rinse in water at the same temperature until clear, and dry in shade.

attrs.title: Iron mordant disposal
attrs.body: Spent iron bath: foul drain only. Never a storm drain, watercourse, soak-away, or compost. Iron sulphate harms aquatic invertebrates.

content.text: Alum mordant (25% WOF) with pomegranate peel at 50% WOF gives a soft warm yellow on wool. Reducing the peel ratio to 30% WOF gives a paler straw-yellow, lighter and cooler. Increasing to 70% WOF deepens the result toward a brass-yellow without markedly changing the colour family.

An iron after-bath (2% WOF iron sulphate) applied after the alum dye-bath shifts the result to a muted olive-green. The olive deepens with longer exposure in the iron bath; remove the wool early for a yellow-green and later for a grey-olive. On unmordanted wool the peel gives a pale beige-tan with no iron, and a soft khaki with an iron after-bath; the tannin alone provides a weak bond.

A second dye bath from the same spent peel is possible. Add fresh hot water to the spent peel, simmer for 30 minutes, and strain. The result on alum-mordanted wool is a paler, softer yellow, useful for a second skein at lower saturation.

items.problem: Colour is pale or uneven on the finished wool
items.cause: Insufficient mordant hold time or the dye-bath temperature dropped below 75°C during dyeing
items.fix: Ensure the mordant bath holds at 80°C for the full 45 minutes. Keep the dye-bath at a consistent 80°C throughout. A probe thermometer makes this straightforward.

items.problem: Yellow came out greenish without the iron after-bath
items.cause: Dye-bath water is hard or has a high pH, which shifts yellow dyes toward green
items.fix: Use rainwater or softened water for the dye bath. A small pinch of cream of tartar (5% WOF) added to the mordant bath can also sharpen the yellow and reduce the green shift.

items.cause: Temperature changes were too abrupt, or the wool was stirred vigorously rather than turned gently
items.fix: Bring the bath up and down in temperature gradually. Turn the wool with a smooth, lifting motion rather than stirring. Never lift cold wool straight into a hot bath.

items.problem: Colour bleeds heavily in the first wash
items.cause: Rinse at the end of dyeing was incomplete, or the mordant was insufficient
items.fix: Rinse until the water runs completely clear. If bleeding continues after two or three rinses, the mordant bath may not have reached the correct temperature. A brief re-mordant and re-dye pass can improve fastness.

content.text: A plain alum and pomegranate peel dye bath is low-hazard at home quantities. Once cooled, the alum bath can go down a foul drain. The spent peel can go into a food-waste or general bin; it will not compost well at home quantities given the high tannin residue.

If an iron after-bath was used, that bath goes to a foul drain only. Never into a storm drain, a watercourse, a soak-away, or onto soil. Iron sulphate harms aquatic invertebrates. For more than 2 litres of spent iron bath, contact your council waste service.
